name: Soft Grotesk L
classification_notes: A rounded neo-grotesque with humanistic proportions, designed as a companion to Mild Grotesk but with softened terminals, placing it clearly in the grotesque tradition with humanist inflection.
summary: A rounded neo-grotesque by Rui Abreu that tempers Swiss-style clarity with humanistic proportions and softened terminals across three optical sizes.
description: Sharing its structural bones with R-Typography's Mild Grotesk, Soft Grotesk swaps the hard terminals for rounded ones, lending warmth without abandoning the assertiveness of Swiss-influenced design. Three static optical sizes — L, M and S — each adjust spacing and x-height to suit display or continuous reading, while a variable font ties those parameters dynamically to body size. Ten styles in the L cut run from ExtraLight to Bold with matching italics.
foundry_blurb: R-Typography describe Soft Grotesk as a rounded sans-serif bridging Neo-Grotesque rationality and humanistic warmth, built as a sibling to Mild Grotesk. The family uses an optical axis across three static sizes — L for tight, high-impact display; S for comfortable reading; and a variable version that adapts proportions and spacing dynamically to body size.

name: Tripsis L
classification_notes: Described as a hybrid between old-style serif and sans-serif, it retains the proportions and rhythm of traditional book typefaces while omitting serifs entirely, placing it in humanist sans territory with a strong calligraphic heritage.
summary: A serif-proportioned sans by Rui Abreu designed for titling, where old-style letterform rhythms are preserved but serifs are deliberately absent.
description: Designed by Rui Abreu and released in 2025, Tripsis L draws on old-style serif proportions while removing the serifs entirely, creating a letterform that sits somewhere between classical book type and contemporary sans. The tight letter spacing is built into its DNA, encouraging the tension between stems to drive visual energy in headline settings. Twelve styles run from ExtraLight to Black with matching italics across a wide range of Latin-extended language support.
foundry_blurb: R-Typography describe Tripsis as a hybrid between old-style and sans-serif, designed specifically for titling with tight letter spacing. They note it is highly reminiscent of traditional serif typefaces made for books, but that the omission of serifs pushes the design toward a more minimalistic appearance and a distinctly different visual rhythm, with stems that can sit closer together and heightened tension between forms.

name: Mild Grotesk L
classification_notes: Described by the designer as bridging Neo-Grotesque rationality and humanistic warmth, with open terminals following natural stroke direction and humanistic proportions that depart from uniform Grotesk rhythm.
summary: A humanist-inflected grotesque by Rui Abreu, available in three optical sizes with round forms and open, stroke-following terminals.
description: Designed by Rui Abreu, Mild Grotesk sits between the rational clarity of Neo-Grotesque and the warmth of humanist sans-serif design. Three static optical sizes, L, M and S, tune spacing and proportions for display impact or continuous reading respectively, while a variable font version synchronises the optical axis dynamically with body size. Terminals follow the natural direction of the stroke rather than meeting a horizontal cut, keeping apertures open throughout the five weights.
foundry_blurb: R-Typography describe Mild Grotesk as an unobtrusive sans-serif that bridges Neo-Grotesque rationality with humanistic warmth, carrying the clarity of the Swiss Style without becoming overly formal. Built on an optical axis, the family ships in three static optical sizes, L, M and S, plus a variable font that adapts proportions and spacing dynamically to ensure optimal rhythm and colour at any body size.

name: Montris L
classification_notes: Montris L is explicitly described as a neoclassical style sitting between Modern (Didone) and Scotch Roman, placing it in the neoclassical or transitional serif tradition with high contrast and refined detailing.
summary: A neoclassical serif by Rui Abreu, optimised for large headlines and titles as the display optical size within the broader Montris family.
description: Montris L is the largest of three optical sizes in Rui Abreu's Montris family, designed specifically for use from 96 point upward where its refined, high-contrast forms read at their best. The family sits stylistically between Modern and Scotch Roman types, offering a digitally precise take on neoclassical form without affectation. Twelve styles cover six weights from ExtraLight to Black, each with a matching italic, making it well suited to magazine and editorial title work.
foundry_blurb: R-Typography describe Montris as a refined, digitally authentic interpretation of the neoclassical style that sits between Modern and the sturdier Scotch Roman types. It is tailored for books and magazines and comprises three optical sizes: S for body text, M for medium-sized headlines, and L for large headlines and titles from 96 point upward.

name: Basilar
classification_notes: Basilar draws directly from the early 20th-century German grotesque Venus, sharing its constrained letter widths and slightly condensed proportions; the systematised glyph widths and mechanised rhythm place it firmly in the rational grotesque tradition.
summary: A rational grotesque by Rui Abreu rooted in the German typeface Venus, with systematised glyph widths that produce a compact, subtly mechanised rhythm.
description: Rui Abreu drew on Venus, the early 20th-century German grotesque that fed into Swiss Style typography, giving Basilar its constrained letter widths and slightly condensed lowercase. The metrics are rigorously systematised so glyphs share a reduced set of widths, lending the text a quietly mechanised cadence without losing the eccentric proportions — notably the generous bowl of the 'a' — that give it character. The family extends to Basilar Mono, where every character shares an identical width, taking the concept to its logical extreme.
foundry_blurb: R-Typography describe Basilar as a rational sans-serif inspired by Venus, the early 20th-century German typeface that influenced Swiss Style sans-serifs. They highlight its constrained letter widths, slightly condensed lowercase, and eccentric proportions, including the generously sized bowl of the 'a', and explain that a meticulous systematisation of metrics creates a compact, subtly mechanised rhythm — a concept pushed furthest in Basilar Mono, where all characters share the same width.

name: Ramboia
classification_notes: Rooted in the French Old-Style tradition with a skeleton inspired by Jean Jannon's 17th-century type, though the design deliberately softens all angles into fluid curves, making it a contemporary and playful reinterpretation of the Garamond/Jannon lineage.
summary: A French Old-Style serif by Rui Abreu that wraps fluid, angle-free curves around a skeleton drawn from Jean Jannon's 17th-century type.
description: Rather than reviving Jannon directly, Ramboia uses his classical skeleton as a hidden armature and traces loose, flowing curves over it, eliminating all angularity for a smooth and relaxed result. The family runs across five weights from Regular to Black, each with a matching italic, and is also available as a variable font. The combination of Old-Style proportions and genuinely soft letterforms makes it equally suited to editorial work that needs warmth and to display settings that call for something more joyful.
foundry_blurb: R-Typography describe Ramboia as a playful and joyful take on the French Old-Style tradition, combining approachability with elegance through five weights crafted entirely from loose, flowing curves free of angles. The design conceals a skeleton inspired by Jean Jannon's 17th-century type, long misattributed to Garamond and later used as the basis for 20th-century revivals. Glyph development was handled by Francisco Torres.

name: Picaresk
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a grotesque, Picaresk draws from 19th-century Egyptian slab faces with serifs removed, retaining their pronounced thick-thin contrast in a sans-serif structure.
summary: A high-contrast grotesque by Rui Abreu that strips the serifs from 19th-century Egyptian faces while preserving their marked thick-thin rhythm.
description: Designed by Rui Abreu and released in 2023, Picaresk takes the structural contrast of Victorian Egyptian typefaces and removes the serifs, leaving thin upstrokes, arms and crossbars set against heavy downstrokes. Six weights from Light to Black each come with matching italics, giving 12 styles in total alongside a variable font. The result sits between classical grotesque tradition and something more characterful, suited to editorial work where contrast carries visual weight.
foundry_blurb: R-Typography describe Picaresk as a grotesque drawing inspiration from robust 19th-century Egyptian faces, but with serifs removed. They highlight its decisive contrast between thicks and thins, with thin upstrokes, arms and crossbars articulated against thick downstrokes, and characterise its personality as poised yet approachable, graduating from a stylish light to an impactful, blocky black.

name: Rizoma L
classification_notes: Rizoma draws directly from Roman monumental inscription letters, inspired by Catich's analysis of the origin of serifs. Its slightly constructivist, utilitarian character places it in a contemporary inscriptional serif subcategory.
summary: A contemporary revival of Roman inscription lettering by Rui Abreu, structured across three optical sizes with six weights and cursive italics.
description: Drawing on Edward M. Catich's research into the stroke patterns underlying imperial Roman lettering, Rizoma adopts a utilitarian and slightly constructivist approach rather than a purely archaeological one. Three optical sizes — S for body text, M from display sizes, L for large-scale titling — each comprise twelve styles spanning ExtraLight to Black with matching italics. Small caps were developed in collaboration with Risto Kujanpää, and the family supports an extensive range of Latin-script languages.
foundry_blurb: R-Typography describe Rizoma as a contemporary typographic interpretation of Roman inscription letters, aimed at capturing the elegance and confidence of the monumental Roman alphabet in a fresh and unpretentious form. The design was inspired by Catich's The Origin of the Serif, which emphasises the everyday writing habits underlying imperial lettering. The result is a utilitarian, slightly constructivist family of text and titling fonts with six weights and energetic cursive italics, available across three optical sizes.

name: Solfa
classification_notes: Flat-sided, spurless and blocky with minimal optical corrections, Solfa is a condensed display face with a self-described brutalist character, placing it firmly outside text categories.
summary: A flat-sided, spurless condensed display face originally drawn for the R-Typography wordmark, carrying a blunt brutalist character.
description: Designed by Catarina Vaz and Rui Abreu, Solfa was built for the R-Typography logo before being released as a three-style family comprising Regular, Italic and Verso. Its blocky proportions, flat sides and absence of spurs produce a sturdy, undecorated texture well suited to headlines and identity work at generous sizes. The minimal optical corrections give it a raw, unpolished quality that recalls vernacular type with a faint historical undertone.
foundry_blurb: R-Typography describe Solfa as a flat-sided, blocky condensed typeface built for strong impact at large sizes. They note its spurless construction and minimal optical corrections as sources of its sturdy aesthetic, and acknowledge its brutalist leanings and raw, straightforward character with a hint of historical flavour. The face was originally drawn as the foundry's own logo type.

name: Azo Sans
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a geometric grotesque, constructed on elementary geometric forms with constructivist 1920s influences, while incorporating humanist qualities that soften its strict geometry.
summary: A geometric grotesque by Rui Abreu that tempers 1920s constructivist rigour with humanist softening to improve readability at text sizes.
description: Built on elementary geometric forms and drawing on constructivist typefaces of the 1920s, Azo Sans introduces humanistic nuances that ease the strictness of pure geometry, making it comfortable to read across longer passages. The family runs to 12 styles across six weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic. Its broad Latin language support extends to a wide range of lesser-resourced scripts and regional varieties.
foundry_blurb: R-Typography describe Azo Sans as a new geometric grotesque loosely based on elementary forms of geometry, constructed in a manner inspired by the constructivist typefaces of the 1920s but instilled with a humanistic quality. The foundry note that the typeface is full of nuances that soften the strictness of pure geometry, making it more human and pleasant to read in longer texts.

name: Atica
classification_notes: Described as German-inspired geometric, explicitly evoking Futura and Paul Renner's proportions, placing it firmly in the geometric sans-serif tradition, with a brutalist inflection from its non-circular, offset-circle O construction.
summary: A geometric sans with a brutalist edge, built on Futura's proportions but distinguished by narrowed, double-circle O constructions that give it a muscular regularity.
description: Designed by Rui Abreu with ExtraLight and Italic masters developed by Francisco Torres, Atica takes the German geometric tradition of Futura as its starting point but departs from circular forms in favour of narrower Os constructed from two vertically offset circles. This produces more regularised stroke widths and a stockier, more forceful appearance than its classical precedents. Ten styles span five weights from ExtraLight to Bold, each with an italic, and a variable font is also available.
foundry_blurb: R-Typography describe Atica as an engineered German-inspired geometric typeface that evokes the vigorous side of Futura while adopting a more brutalist appearance. Though not entirely departing from the classical proportions of Paul Renner's design, the typeface features Os that are not circular but narrower, constructed with two vertically offset circles, resulting in slightly more regularised widths and an overall muscular look.

name: Canora
classification_notes: Canora is rooted in English round hand calligraphy, a formal copperplate-derived script tradition. Its mechanical interpretation with straight lines and abrupt cuts pushes it toward stylised display script rather than orthodox calligraphy.
summary: A mechanised take on English round hand calligraphy in two styles, one upright and one left-leaning, by Rui Abreu.
description: Rui Abreu pushes the English round hand tradition toward a stylised, mechanical aesthetic by substituting fluid calligraphic transitions with straight lines, abrupt cuts and geometric loops. Canora Frente is the upright style, while Canora Verso tilts to the left at an unusual angle, testing how far the script model can absorb arbitrary design decisions before it ceases to read as calligraphy. Condensed lowercase letters contrast with more decorative, swash-laden capitals across a broad Latin Extended character set.
foundry_blurb: R-Typography describe Canora Frente as a robust interpretation of the English round hand, a style that is fluid and graceful yet surprisingly structured, pushed here toward a modernised and mechanical look through straight lines, abrupt cuts and stylised loops and dots. Canora Verso further tests the model with an unusual left-leaning angle. The pair shares condensed lowercase letters and moderately opulent majuscules with swashes and ample curves.

name: Staff Grotesk
classification_notes: A text-oriented grotesque sans with wide spacing and open counters, positioned within the European sans serif tradition and designed separately from the display-oriented Staff family.
summary: A text-oriented grotesque by Rui Abreu that strips back the expressive qualities of the original Staff family in favour of readability at smaller sizes.
description: Designed separately from the original Staff (2019), Staff Grotesk retains a handful of the parent family's idiosyncrasies, including a large-bowled lowercase 'a', pointed apex on the capital M and a vertically-cut tail on the Q, but frames them within a more conventional European grotesque structure. Wider spacing, open counters and a reduced range of four weights from Light to Bold orient it squarely towards continuous text rather than display use. Eight styles are available, each with a matching italic, along with a variable font.
foundry_blurb: R-Typography describe Staff Grotesk as a new text-oriented take on the multi-width Staff family, focused on readability in small sizes and longer passages of text. They note it has a seemingly low-key presence, slightly more attuned to the European sans serif tradition, while remaining subtly dotted with reminiscences of the original quirks of Staff's large-size letterforms.

name: Chassi L
classification_notes: Chassi is a revival of the Garamond genre, drawing on the elegant roman types of Claude Garamond, Robert Granjon and Jean Jannon. Its high contrast and sharp details in the L optical size place it firmly in the old-style humanist serif tradition.
summary: A Garamond revival designed as a genre study rather than a strict historical facsimile, with three optical variants tuned for different size ranges from body text to large display.
description: Designed by Rui Abreu, Chassi L is the display-optimised optical cut of the Chassi family, intended for use at 96 point and above, where its high contrast, tight spacing and sharp details read at their best. The family as a whole draws on Claude Garamond, Robert Granjon and Jean Jannon as collective reference points rather than pursuing fidelity to any single source. Ten styles across five weights from ExtraLight to Bold, each with a matching italic, cover an extensive range of Latin-script languages.
foundry_blurb: R-Typography describe Chassi as an editorial type family designed for fluency in extended texts, taking Garamond as a genre rather than a single historical model and incorporating influences from Granjon and Jannon. The three optical series S, M and L are calibrated for progressively larger size ranges, with the L optimised for 96 point and above, offering higher contrast, tighter spacing and sharper details than the text-focused S.

name: Gineto
classification_notes: A contemporary grotesque with explicit lineage from 19th-century American Gothic no. 4 and influences from Akzidenz Grotesk, placing it firmly in the grotesque sans-serif tradition with humanist undertones from OCR B.
summary: A contemporary grotesque by Rui Abreu drawn from the brevier cut of Gothic no. 4, designed for small sizes with open forms, generous spacing and long ascenders.
description: Rui Abreu derived Gineto from the brevier size of Gothic no. 4, an 1800s design from New York's Farmer, Little & Co., retaining its narrow proportions, generous letterspacing and elongated extenders. Open counters prevent the ambiguity common in closed grotesque letterforms, and input from Frutiger's OCR B adds legibility precision, while a nod to Akzidenz Grotesk keeps the European sans tradition audible. The family comes in two sub-families, Gineto and Gineto Fit, which share the same design but swap the default length of ascenders and descenders, with stylistic alternates providing the opposite set in each.
foundry_blurb: R-Typography describe Gineto as a clean and airy contemporary grotesque tailored for small sizes, developed from the brevier of Gothic no. 4 by Farmer, Little & Co. They note its spacious, ventilated construction and deliberately unambiguous letterforms, citing the elongated dot of the i as a particularly striking inherited detail. Alongside Gothic no. 4, the design drew from Frutiger's OCR B for legibility and from Akzidenz Grotesk for its European sensibility.

name: Sul Tripla
classification_notes: A triple-line constructivist variant of Sul Sans, exposing the geometric skeleton of each letterform through parallel strokes. Classified as display due to its decorative, structural nature unsuited to running text.
summary: A triple-line constructivist spin-off of Sul Sans that makes the geometric construction of each letter visually explicit through parallel strokes.
description: Part of the Sul Effects group alongside Sul Dupla and Sul Stencil, this single-style display face strips Sul Sans back to its geometric skeleton and redraws each letter as three parallel lines. The effect foregrounds construction over legibility, making it suited to headlines, logotypes and graphic contexts where structure is the point. Designed by Rui Abreu and Catarina Vaz and released in 2018.
foundry_blurb: R-Typography describe Sul Tripla as one of three constructivist spin-offs of Sul Sans collectively called Sul Effects, alongside Sul Dupla and Sul Stencil. They note that these variants emphasise the construction of the original Sul Sans by exposing the geometric elements that form its letters.

name: Staff X Wide
classification_notes: Staff X Wide is explicitly described as neo-grotesque in tradition, with mechanical, dry letterforms and structural rigidity characteristic of that genre. The X Wide variant is the broadest width in a large multi-width family.
summary: The extra-wide cut of Rui Abreu's Staff family, a neo-grotesque with rigid geometry and subtly offbeat detailing across 12 styles.
description: Staff X Wide sits at the broadest end of an 82-style family spanning seven widths and six weights with matching 14-degree slanted italics. Letterforms follow the neo-grotesque tradition but carry distinctive touches: a pointed apex on the M, an enlarged bowl on the a, and a vertically-cut tail on the Q. Terminals are cut horizontally at a consistent height across letters and weights, and vertical metrics are shared across the entire family.
foundry_blurb: R-Typography describe Staff as a sturdy neo-grotesque sans designed for practical, space-conscious use. The family's mechanical and dry character comes from right-angled bends, horizontally cut terminals aligned across different letters and weights, and crossbars and waists held at consistent vertical positions throughout all 82 styles.

name: Flecha L
classification_notes: Flecha is described as an old-style typeface with square terminals, geometrically simplified strokes and broad-nib pen references, placing it firmly in the old-style serif tradition with a mechanised, contemporary inflection.
summary: The large optical size of Rui Abreu's Flecha family, designed for headlines and titles from 84 point upward in editorial contexts.
description: Flecha L is the display-optimised cut of Rui Abreu's Flecha, an old-style serif built for editorial design with rigid letterforms, square terminals and geometrically simplified strokes that retain a faint trace of the broad-nib pen. The family spans three optical sizes — S for body text, M for mid-scale headlines, L for large display — with Flecha L offering 12 styles across six weights from ExtraLight to Black, each with a matching italic. Picked up by Afar, Billboard, Esquire and Max Magazine, it has established itself as a credible editorial serif in the years since its 2019 release.
foundry_blurb: R-Typography describe Flecha as a sharp and streamlined old-style typeface made for editorial design, with a sturdy, mechanised appearance defined by rigid letterforms, square terminals and geometrically simplified strokes, yet remaining delicate and reminiscent of the broad-nib pen. The family is divided into three optical sizes — S for body text, M for medium headlines, and L for large headlines and titles from 84 point upward.

name: Plural
classification_notes: Plural blends the sleek, streamlined shapes typical of branding-oriented sans-serifs with a slight formal and historical flavour more common to editorial faces, placing it within the humanist sans tradition.
summary: A twelve-style sans by Rui Abreu that balances formal editorial qualities with the clean, streamlined shapes suited to branding work.
description: Designed by Rui Abreu in 2017, Plural spans six weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, covering branding and editorial applications. Its defining characteristic is a dual-form lowercase g, which toggles between a more traditional double-storey form and a simpler single-storey version, giving designers a handle on how formal or neutral the type feels. The Latin character set extends across a wide range of languages including African and Pacific scripts written in Latin orthographies.
foundry_blurb: R-Typography describe Plural as a clean, multi-purpose sans serif built for both branding and editorial. They note it combines a slight formal and historical flavour, typical of editorial typefaces, with the sleek and streamlined shapes sought in branding contexts, a duality made explicit through two available forms of the lowercase g.

name: Gliko Modern L
classification_notes: Gliko Modern sits between Dutch Old Style and Didot rationalism: high stroke contrast, unbracketed and wedge-shaped serifs mixed, large x-height and mixed stress place it firmly in transitional territory with Didone leanings.
summary: The large optical size of Rui Abreu's Gliko Modern, designed for headlines and display settings from 96 points upward.
description: Gliko Modern L is the large optical cut of Rui Abreu's editorial serif, optimised for display and title use from 96 points. The design tensions Dutch Old Style sturdiness against Didot-era rationalisation, combining high stroke contrast with a mixture of unbracketed and wedge-shaped serifs, a generous x-height and mixed stress axis. Ball terminals, common to both historical models, are deliberately absent. Ten styles run across five weights from Light to Black, each with a matching italic.
foundry_blurb: R-Typography describe Gliko Modern as a design that moves between the sturdy elegance of late 17th and 18th-century Dutch Old Style types and the delicacy and extreme rationalisation of Didot. The family features high stroke contrast, thin unbracketed serifs mixed with wedge-shaped ones, a large x-height and mixed stress, while consciously avoiding the ball terminals common to both historical sources. It is divided into three optical sizes: S for body text, M for medium headlines, and L for large headlines and titles.

name: Aquino
classification_notes: Classified as display due to its stencil-like calligraphic construction derived from 18th-century liturgical manuscript lettering; the unconnected strokes and serifed letterforms place it firmly in decorative display territory rather than conventional text use.
summary: A display stencil typeface drawn from an 18th-century Portuguese Cistercian manuscript, with serifed letterforms built on unconnected calligraphic strokes.
description: Derived from the 1735 liturgical manuscript Collectaneum Sacri Ordinis Cisterciensis by Friar Tomás Aquino, the typeface translates his consistent, unconnected penwork into stencil-like display letterforms. The lowercase follows the manuscript's original minuscule structures directly, while the uppercase was largely redrawn for structural consistency with the lowercase logic rather than reproducing Aquino's ornate majuscules. Available in five weights from Thin to Bold, it carries an unusual historical specificity rare in display type.
foundry_blurb: R-Typography describe Aquino as a display calligraphic stencil typeface inspired by a liturgical book made by Portuguese Cistercian friar Tomás Aquino in 1735. The friar's handwriting showed a distinctive typographic character through uniform, consistent serifed letterforms written with unconnected strokes, producing naturally stencil-like letters. The design follows his minuscule structures closely while reimagining the uppercase for consistency with the lowercase rather than reproducing his decorative originals.

name: Sul Sans
classification_notes: Geometrically constructed with minimal optical corrections, drawing on vernacular letterforms observed on Portuguese signage and façades; sits within the geometric sans tradition but with a deliberately undesigned, idiosyncratic character.
summary: A geometrically constructed sans drawn from capital letterforms observed on Portuguese signage and façades, deliberately resisting classical optical corrections.
description: Designed by Rui Abreu and released in 2016, Sul Sans takes cues from the strict, idiosyncratic geometry found in lettering on Portuguese buildings and street signage. The family runs across five weights from Light to Black, each with a matching italic, totalling ten styles. Minimal optical adjustment is a deliberate choice, preserving the undesigned, vernacular quality that distinguishes the typeface from more polished geometric sans families.
foundry_blurb: R-Typography describe Sul Sans as a geometrically constructed typeface incorporating quirks observed in capital letters on signage and façades throughout Portugal. They note that these features are not exclusively Portuguese but appear wherever letterforms are subjected to strict geometry without regard for classical proportions, and that Sul Sans employs minimal optical adjustments to capture a vernacular, undesigned spirit.

name: Grifo L
classification_notes: Grifo L exhibits extreme stroke contrast drawn from neoclassical models, with bracketed serifs and sharp triangular terminals that give it a precise, scalpel-cut rational character distinct from conventional Didone types.
summary: A high-contrast neoclassical serif by Rui Abreu, cut with sharp triangular terminals and talon-shaped curves, optimised for display and large-scale use.
description: Grifo L is the display optical size of Rui Abreu's Grifo family, which draws on neoclassical stroke contrast while adding bracketed serifs and emphatic sharp spikes throughout the letterforms. The design carries a deliberately rational quality: vertical strokes end in abrupt horizontal cuts and baseline serifs on several letters are removed to sharpen and tighten the overall texture. Ten styles run from Light to Black with matching italics, and the family has seen use in publications including Texas Monthly, Fast Company and M Le Magazine du Monde.
foundry_blurb: R-Typography describe Grifo as drawing on the extreme stroke contrast of neoclassic types, with bracketed serifs and sharp triangular terminals. Talon shapes appear throughout, particularly in the c, e, t, commas and quotes, and the overall approach is described as prominently rational, as though letters were cut with a scalpel. The L size is recommended for display use from 132 point upward, while the S and M sizes serve smaller optical ranges.

name: Pathos
classification_notes: Pathos is explicitly a slab serif, defined by its extreme rectangular serifs and terminals pushed to the physical limits of legibility. The design concept centres entirely on maximising serif size, placing it firmly in the slab category with an experimental, display-oriented character.
summary: A slab serif by Rui Abreu built around one constraint: pushing rectangular serifs to the absolute limit of available space.
description: Designed by Rui Abreu in 2016, Pathos takes a single design premise and follows it to its logical extreme, maximising serif size until the letterforms themselves must adapt, leaning left or right to accommodate the uncompromisingly thick rectangular serifs and terminals. The result is an unusual structural dynamism that gives each glyph a distinct posture while maintaining legibility in both text and headline settings. Twelve styles span six weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic.
foundry_blurb: R-Typography describe Pathos as a slab serif built on a simple idea: make the serifs as large as physically possible. The constraints imposed by letter spacing and internal counters force the letterforms into dynamic, sometimes asymmetric postures, lending the typeface a distinct personality alongside surprising clarity and legibility across both text and display use.
